questions.q: The rule of law holds that all persons and authorities are subject to what?
questions.explanation: The rule of law means everyone, including authorities, is subject to and accountable under the law.

questions.q: A central idea of the rule of law is that the law is applied how?
questions.explanation: The rule of law requires that the law be applied equally to all persons.

questions.q: The rule of law is regarded as part of which feature of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The rule of law is regarded as part of the basic structure of the Indian Constitution.

questions.q: Which Article guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the laws?
questions.explanation: Article 14 guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the laws, embodying the rule of law.

questions.q: Under the rule of law, can the government act against a citizen without legal authority?
options: No, government action must have legal authority
questions.explanation: The rule of law requires that government action be backed by legal authority, not arbitrary will.

questions.q: The opposite of the rule of law is best described as what?
questions.explanation: The rule of law stands in contrast to arbitrary or absolute power exercised without legal limits.

questions.q: Which Article protects life and personal liberty, stating no person shall be deprived of them except according to procedure established by law?
questions.explanation: Article 21 protects life and personal liberty, requiring deprivation only by lawful procedure, central to the rule of law.

questions.q: Under the rule of law, who guards against unlawful government action by interpreting and applying the law?
questions.explanation: An independent judiciary upholds the rule of law by checking unlawful government action.

questions.q: The supremacy of the Constitution over all organs of government is an aspect of which principle?
questions.explanation: Constitutional supremacy, under which all organs act within constitutional limits, is an aspect of the rule of law.

questions.q: The right to move the Supreme Court to enforce fundamental rights, supporting the rule of law, is guaranteed by which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 32 guarantees the right to constitutional remedies, enabling citizens to enforce the rule of law in the Supreme Court.

questions.q: The classic exposition of the rule of law in modern legal thought is associated with which jurist?
questions.explanation: The modern formulation of the rule of law is associated with the British jurist A.V. Dicey.

questions.q: Dicey's conception of the rule of law included the supremacy of law, equality before the law, and what third element?
options: The rule of a single monarch
The predominance of the legal spirit through court decisions
questions.explanation: Dicey's third element was that constitutional principles result from court decisions defining the rights of individuals.

questions.q: Article 14's guarantee against arbitrary State action was notably expanded in which 1978 case?
options: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
questions.explanation: In Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978), the Court held that Article 14 strikes at arbitrariness, deepening the rule of law.

questions.q: In which 1980 case did the Supreme Court describe the rule of law as a basic feature of the Constitution?
options: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
questions.explanation: In Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980), the Court reaffirmed the rule of law as part of the basic structure.

questions.q: Equality before the law in Article 14 is said to be borrowed from which legal tradition?
questions.explanation: The phrase 'equality before the law' reflects the British common law tradition associated with Dicey.

questions.q: Article 13 declares that laws inconsistent with which Part of the Constitution are void, reinforcing the rule of law?
questions.explanation: Article 13 makes laws inconsistent with the Fundamental Rights in Part III void, upholding legal supremacy.

questions.q: Which Article provides protection against ex-post-facto laws, double jeopardy and self-incrimination, supporting fair legal process?
questions.explanation: Article 20 guarantees protection in respect of conviction for offences, a safeguard tied to the rule of law.

questions.q: Which Article guarantees protection against arbitrary arrest and detention, including the right to be informed of grounds of arrest?
questions.explanation: Article 22 provides safeguards against arbitrary arrest and detention, reinforcing the rule of law.

questions.q: An independent judiciary, essential to the rule of law, is protected partly by security of tenure for judges under which scheme?
options: Election by the public every year
Fixed grounds and procedure for removal in the Constitution
Approval by the Council of Ministers monthly
questions.explanation: Judges enjoy security of tenure, removable only on specified grounds and procedure, safeguarding judicial independence.

questions.q: High Courts can issue writs to enforce the rule of law under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue writs, an important tool for enforcing the rule of law.

questions.q: The principle that even the highest authorities are accountable under the law means that the State can be sued for unlawful acts, a doctrine related to what?
questions.explanation: The rule of law supports State accountability, allowing legal remedies against unlawful State action.

questions.q: The phrase 'equal protection of the laws' in Article 14 is associated with which constitutional tradition?
questions.explanation: The phrase 'equal protection of the laws' is drawn from the American constitutional tradition.

questions.q: In which 1973 case did the Supreme Court lay down the basic structure doctrine, of which the rule of law is considered a part?
options: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine, which includes the rule of law, was laid down in Kesavananda Bharati (1973).

questions.q: In the Indira Nehru Gandhi election case of 1975, the rule of law was upheld as a basic feature when the Court struck down a clause excluding what from judicial scrutiny?
options: The dispute over a specific election
questions.explanation: In the 1975 election case, the Court held that placing an election dispute beyond judicial scrutiny violated the rule of law.

questions.q: The expression 'rule of law' is sometimes traced in legal history to which medieval English document limiting the power of the Crown?
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta of 1215 is often cited as an early milestone limiting arbitrary power, an antecedent of the rule of law.

questions.q: In Indian constitutional doctrine, the rule of law is closely linked to the prohibition of arbitrariness, a link emphasised in interpreting which Article?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court linked the rule of law to non-arbitrariness in its interpretation of Article 14.

questions.q: The administrative law principle that powers must be exercised for proper purposes and within legal limits flows from which broader doctrine?
questions.explanation: Limits on administrative discretion to prevent abuse of power derive from the rule of law.

questions.q: The maxim that 'no person is above the law' captures which aspect of the rule of law as Dicey explained it?
options: Equality before the law for officials and citizens alike
questions.explanation: Dicey's equality element means officials and ordinary citizens are equally subject to the ordinary law and courts.

questions.q: The remedy of habeas corpus, by which courts examine the legality of a person's detention, directly upholds the rule of law and is available under which Articles?
questions.explanation: Habeas corpus can be sought under Articles 32 and 226, enabling courts to test the legality of any detention.

questions.q: The doctrine that a law must be clear, certain and prospective so that people can know and follow it is an element of which principle?
questions.explanation: Clarity, certainty and prospective operation of laws are considered essential elements of the rule of law.
